questionText: Given that $5\sin\theta + 12\cos\theta = 13\sin(\theta+\alpha)$ where $\alpha = 67.4^\circ$ (to $1$ d.p.), find the maximum value of $5\sin\theta + 12\cos\theta$ and the value of $\theta$ in $0^\circ\le\theta<360^\circ$ at which this maximum occurs.

steps.explanation: The expression has already been written as a single sine wave, so its behaviour is easy to read off. We work directly from this form.

steps.description: State the range of the sine factor
steps.explanation: A sine function can never be larger than $1$ or smaller than $-1$, no matter what its angle is. This fact alone controls the size of the whole expression.
Multiplying the bounds by the positive amplitude $13$ shows the expression stays between $-13$ and $13$. No calculus is needed; the amplitude gives the extreme values directly.
The greatest value the expression can reach is $13$, achieved exactly when the sine factor equals its largest value, $1$.

steps.description: Set the sine factor equal to 1
steps.explanation: The sine of an angle equals $1$ when that angle is $90^\circ$. Setting the shifted angle to $90^\circ$ locates the maximum.
Subtracting the phase shift $67.4^\circ$ undoes it and gives the value of $\theta$ where the maximum occurs.

steps.description: Confirm θ lies in the interval
steps.explanation: The solution $22.6^\circ$ lies inside the required interval, so it is the value of $\theta$ at which the expression reaches its maximum.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The maximum value is $13$, occurring at $\theta = 22.6^\circ$.
